mnemonics.title: A* Formula Components
mnemonics.content: Remember: Fido Gets His Bone - f(n) = g(n) + h(n): 'g' for Ground (actual path cost from start), 'h' for Home (heuristic home/goal estimate), 'f' for Final (total estimated cost)

mnemonics.title: Admissible Heuristic
mnemonics.content: Admissible = Always Optimistic (never overestimates). Remember: A for Admissible = Always underestimate or equal (never go over)

mnemonics.title: Greedy vs A*
mnemonics.content: Greedy is 'Goo-Goo' (only looks at Goodness/goal), A* is 'All-in' (looks at All: actual cost + goal estimate). Greedy is Fast but Foolish, A* is Accurate but costs Memory.

acronyms.usage: Best-first search algorithm combining g(n) + h(n)
Estimates cost from node n to goal
Actual cost from start to node n
Estimated total solution cost through node n = g(n) + h(n)
Frontier - nodes generated but not yet expanded
Explored set - nodes that have been fully expanded
Memory-efficient variant of A* using iterative deepening

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a priority queue as a funnel where nodes with lowest f(n) values drop to the front. The OPEN set is like a to-do list sorted by 'promisingness' - nodes closest to solution come first.
Think of h(n) as a GPS estimate. In a city, Manhattan distance is like 'as the crow flies' but constrained to roads. It guides you toward destination but doesn't know about traffic (obstacles).
Greedy is like someone who only looks at destination direction and walks blindly. A* is like someone who checks both distance traveled AND remaining distance - more reliable.
Think of a journey: g(n) = fuel used so far, h(n) = estimated fuel to destination, f(n) = total estimated fuel needed. A* picks routes minimizing total estimated fuel.

analogies.analogy: Like using both a map (heuristic) and odometer (actual distance). You choose routes that minimize estimated total distance, not just what looks closest to destination.
Like a rumor or estimate - it gives you a hint about how far you are from your goal, but might not be perfectly accurate. A good heuristic is like well-informed gossip.
Like a tourist who only asks 'which way to the monument?' without considering how far they've already walked or route conditions.
Like an optimistic friend who never overestimates travel time - they might say 'it's only 10 minutes' when it's actually 15, but never say 'it's 5 minutes' when it's 20.
